    [Feedback] App Directory Beta #41745

    Closed Locked Answered by leerob
    leerob asked this question in Help
    [Feedback] App Directory Beta #41745
    leerob
    Oct 24, 2022 · 831 comments · 1,785 replies
    Answered by leerob Return to top
    Discussion options

      • Uh oh!

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

      • Uh oh!

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

    leerob
    Oct 24, 2022

    -

    Next.js 13 introduces the app directory (beta) with new features and conventions. However, upgrading to Next.js 13 does not require using the app directory.

    This discussion is where you can provide feedback on the new conventions and features around layouts, server components, streaming, and more.

    If you have feedback on Turbopack, please use this discussion.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    You must be logged in to vote
    All reactions
    • 👍 120
    • 😄 10
    • 🎉 60
    • 😕 7
    • ❤️ 85
    • 🚀 52
    • 👀 51
    Answered by leerob May 4, 2023

    App Router Stability

    Hey folks, exciting news. The Next.js App Router is now stable!

    We couldn't have gotten without the literally thousands of replies in this discussion helping us uncover bugs, patch issues, and iterate on our design based on your feedback. We greatly appreciate it. Our focus for the coming weeks will be on fixing any additional bugs that come up during this new wave of feedback.

    Now that the router is stable, there are more additions we want to make, many based on the feedback shared here. To help make it easier to track feedback and upvote or discuss specific feature requests moving forward, we're going to close out this discussion.

    We've made a new section in Discuss…

    View full answer

    icopp
    Oct 25, 2022

    -

    For me, the one big remaining question is how to handle form POSTs in this new structure. My company has interaction with the server built around progressively enhanced form handling (currently using next-runtime to get a more Remix-like experience), and this is one of the things I was hoping would be front and center, but so far it seems to be conspicuously missing.

    Looking forward to this! Ideally, we could define mutations on the server and we can import them just like that on the client side and it works with typing and everything. Maybe something like https://github.com/Janpot/next-rpc would be awesome.

    icopp Nov 2, 2022

    -

    I think the ideal here would really be to have mutations and manually-triggered revalidations fundamentally use form POST/GET (as appropriate), so that you can have a page that works fine even with JS disabled. This would mean adding some handling around, e.g. form values being limited to strings, but I don't think that's really a hard blocker on anything. The need to route everything through client-side JS handling is IMO the biggest weakness with Next pre-13.x, and it would seem like a huge missed opportunity to me to not correct that with all the other emphasis on server-side handling.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    ggonchar Dec 11, 2022

    -

    @timneutkens I guess we are all excited to learn how the improved mutations and forms handle guidance will look (any teasers? ;)) I found using the RPC approach here quite promising. Both Server Components and Client Components using RPC have the same coding style when server-side functions can be just used directly inside React components. Let me share a quick example and a post to articulate the idea. Curious what you all think

    AliMejbar Feb 10, 2023

    -

    Mutations are the biggest question left unanswered by the new app directory for me. For an app, as opposed to a what some would call a "standard website", mutating data is as important, if not more so, than fetching it. The temporary workaround in the documentation feels sketchy. Moreover, the "beta" status is causing major API libraries (such as TRPC and Apollo GraphQL) to not attempt definitive implementations for fear of breaking changes. I don't mean to sound demanding, but I strongly believe that implementing mutations in an effective manner should be a top priority.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    juanferreras
    Oct 25, 2022

    -

    RE: On-demand Revalidation, there's a call out in Data Fetching > Revalidating Data, but it doesn't seem to appear listed elsewhere (nor on Roadmap).

    It'd be great to understand the plan for On-demand Revalidation on the /app/ directory, whether eventually we could force-update a single segment (like we can do today in /pages/), many segments separately, a layout's own data, etc. Or whether it's not really planned in the near term and shouldn't be relied upon.

    Many thanks!

    timneutkens Oct 26, 2022
    Maintainer

    -

    This might help explain the rendering equivalents a bit: https://beta.nextjs.org/docs/rendering/fundamentals

    Right now on-demand revalidation already works, if the page uses static rendering which is the case when you don't have a bailout on the page like calling cookies, headers, or a fetch with cache: 'no-store' you can on-demand revalidate that page.

    In the future this will be expanded to be more granular than per-page deciding static rendering or dynamic rendering.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    airtonix Apr 25, 2023

    -

    I actually don't like the lmao lets put everything that's not a route thing in the route things folder ...

    everything in pages/* should be a browser router. in fact I go as far to say that there should only be "routing" paradigms in the the pages folder.

    everything else should be imported from outside the pages folder 👍🏻

    components/
  landing/
    HomPage/
      index.ts
      styles.css.ts
      HomePage.tsx
      HomePageContainer.tsx
      HomePage.stories.tsx
      HomePage.test.tsx
    LandingCard/
      index.ts
      styles.css.ts
      LandingCard.tsx
      LandingCardContainer.tsx
      LandingCard.test.tsx
      LandingCard.stories.tsx
    ...

  otherarea/
    ...etc

services/
  Auth/
    index.ts
    ...
  Theme/
    index.ts
    ...
  Cms/
    index.ts
    ...

config/
  index.ts

pages/
  index.tsx

    That the nextjs team encourage putting everthing in the app directory feels like shortsighted thinking.

    Andarist Nov 29, 2022

    -

    I got a bug report for Emotion recently that showcased to me how a Remix app gets broken with somebody's web extension. The root cause behind this that I've diagnosed is the same thing that is being discussed here - rehydrating the whole <html/>.

    I'm not exactly sure what the real extension was trying to do. I only got a minimal repro that had this in the extension:

    document.documentElement.appendChild(document.createElement('foo'))

    Perhaps the issue reporters could shed some more light on what they are trying to achieve there to get some advice here. cc @flappyBug @lucascurti

    While I agree that a lot of things can break the reconciliation - a custom container used as root was always a good enough boundary that allowed 3rd party things to work most of the time.

    I'm also a little bit confused when it comes to useServerInsertedHTML. Things inserted when rendering the shell~ are inserted into <head/> and I'm not sure what kind of rule makes this OK here since that's bypassing React. How those extra <head/> children are OK? 🤔 cc @huozhi

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    All reactions
    • 👍 2
    Comment options

      • Uh oh!

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

    hourann Nov 29, 2022

    -

    The original issue @Andarist mentioned is here. I got report that some browser extension breaks our site style, including Urban VPN Proxy. I reviewed the compiled extension code, and reproduced a minimal extension to make this happen. The key point is that the extension injects new DOM element into the document in a content script runs at document_start

    Another fun fact is to break React 18's hydrateRoot, one additional DOM element is enough. But to break the legacy hydrate, the extension need to append at least 2 DOM elements (no matter what).

    karlhorky Feb 26, 2023

    -

    API Routes are now supported in the app/ directory since version 13.2.0 🎉 They are called Route Handlers and the convention is route.ts:

    • Announcement blog post: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-13-2#custom-route-handlers
    • Docs: https://beta.nextjs.org/docs/routing/route-handlers

    Does that mean that with the app folder it will no longer be possible to do domain-level-localization

    No, you get full flexibility for whichever approach you want to take by leveraging middleware.js. Middleware allows you to rewrite an incoming hostname to a different path which is how that config option in Next.js does it.

    Could you elaborate on how to exactly include localized text context with that approach or direct me to a resource?

    mike14747 Feb 5, 2023

    -

    I know this thread is a little old, but it didn't seem like anyone gave an answer to the question of how to include <SessionProvider> into the appDir layout.tsx (other than to make it a client component).

    I do server-side data fetching in layout.tsx, so I wanted to keep it a server component. Here's how I've addressed this...

    I created a client component called <ClientSessionProvider>:

    'use client';

import { ReactNode } from 'react';
import { Session } from 'next-auth';
import { SessionProvider } from 'next-auth/react';

type ClientSessionProps = {
    children: ReactNode;
    session: Session;
}

export default function ClientSession({ children, session }: ClientSessionProps) {
    return (
        <SessionProvider session={session}>
            {children}
        </SessionProvider>
    );
}

    Then I imported it into my layout.tsx (which can still be a server component) and use it just like I'd have used the real <SessionProvider>. useSession() and getServerSession() are available in all my pages/components it wraps.

    return (
        <html lang='en'>
            <head />
            <body id="appWrapper">
                <ClientSessionProvider session={session}>
                    <SkipToMain />
                    <Header topInfoActive={settingsData?.topInfoActive} topInfoText={settingsData?.topInfoText} />
                    <Navbar />

                    <div className="page-container">
                        {children}
                        <ScrollTop />
                    </div>

                    <Footer contactEmail={settingsData?.contactEmail} />
                </ClientSessionProvider>
            </body>
        </html>
    );

    Not sure why, but depending upon which version of typescript I'm using, typescript may complain that session is not a valid layout prop.

    mike14747 Mar 23, 2023

    -

    Yeah, you will be able to use useSession() in client components and getServerSession() in server components.

    Example of client component usage (my Authbar component):

    'use client';

import Link from 'next/link';
import { signOut, useSession } from 'next-auth/react';
import Button from '../components/Button';

import styles from '../../styles/Authbar.module.css';

export default function Authbar() {
    const { data: session, status } = useSession();
    const loading = status === 'loading';

    return (
        <section className={styles.authbarContainer}>
            {loading && <>Loading...</>}

            {!session && !loading &&
                <p>
                    <Link href="/login?callbackUrl=/">
                        Login
                    </Link>

                    <span>&nbsp;/&nbsp;</span>

                    <Link href={'/register'}>
                        Register
                    </Link>
                </p>
            }

            {session?.user &&
                <>
                    <span className={styles.username}>
                        <>User: </>
                        <Link href="/profile">
                            {session.user.name}
                        </Link>
                    </span>

                    <Button onClick={() => signOut({ callbackUrl: '/' })} size="small" variant="text">Logout</Button>
                </>
            }
        </section>
    );
}

    bpossolo Mar 30, 2023

    -

    @devHybrid0306
    your layouts should be server components as much as possible.
    you can have middleware that creates the web session and makes it available on inbound request as a cookie.
    then your page/layout can extract the session from the request cookies.
    you can also have middleware guarding your pages
    below you can see what I use in my app.
    nextjs uses the primary middleware.ts file which creates a pipeline of supplementary middleware filters that i've defined.

    // /middleware.ts
import { NextResponse, NextMiddleware } from 'next/server';
import { SessionFactory } from './app/middleware/session-factory.ts';
import { AuthPageGuard } from './app/middleware/auth-page-guard';
import { foldLeft } from './utils/function.utils';

const factories = [
  SessionFactory,
  AuthPageGuard,
];
const base: NextMiddleware = () => NextResponse.next();
export const middleware = foldLeft(
  (factory, accumulation) => factory(accumulation),
  base,
  factories
);
    // /app/middleware/middleware-factory.ts
import { NextMiddleware } from 'next/server';
export type MiddlewareFactory = (middleware: NextMiddleware) => NextMiddleware;
    // /app/middleware/session-factory.ts

import { NextMiddleware, NextResponse } from 'next/server';
import { v4 } from 'uuid';
import { expiration, SESSION_ID } from '../../utils/cookie.utils';
import { routes } from '../../utils/route.utils';
import { MiddlewareFactory } from './middleware-factory';

// creates session and adds session id to inbound request cookies if one doesn't already exist
// stores session id as cookie on response if it doesn't already exist
export const SessionFactory: MiddlewareFactory = (next: NextMiddleware) => {
  const middleware: NextMiddleware = async (request, event) => {
    const secure = request.nextUrl.protocol.startsWith('https');
    const path = request.nextUrl.pathname;
    const match = routes.pages.patterns.some(pattern => pattern.test(path));
    if (!match) {
      return next(request, event);
    }
    const cookie = request.cookies.get(SESSION_ID);
    const sessionId = cookie ? cookie.value : v4();
    if (!cookie) {
      request.cookies.set(SESSION_ID, sessionId);
    }
    const result = await next(request, event);
    if (!cookie && result instanceof NextResponse) {
      result.cookies.set({
        name: SESSION_ID,
        value: sessionId,
        expires: expiration('years', 1),
        secure,
      });
    }
    return result;
  };
  return middleware;
}
    // /app/middleware/auth-guard.ts
import { NextMiddleware, NextResponse } from 'next/server';
import { routes } from '../../utils/route.utils';
import { MiddlewareFactory } from './middleware-factory';

/**
  * request to /login or /register which already has an access-token is redirected to home page
  */
export const AuthPageGuard: MiddlewareFactory = (next: NextMiddleware) => {
  const patterns = [
    routes.auth.register.pattern,
    routes.auth.login.pattern,
  ];
  const middleware: NextMiddleware = (request, event) => {
    const path = request.nextUrl.pathname;
    const match = patterns.some(pattern => pattern.test(path));
    if (match && request.cookies.has('access-token')) {
      const parts = path.split('/');
      const locale = parts[1];
      const base = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_BASE_URL;
      const url = routes.home.url(locale);
      const location = `${base}${url}`;
      return NextResponse.redirect(location, 303);
    }
    return next(request, event);
  }
  return middleware;
}
    // /utils/function.utils.ts
export function foldLeft<I, A>(folder: (item: I, accumulation: A) => A, accumulation: A, items: I[]): A {
  if (items.length === 0) {
    return accumulation;
  }
  const [item, ...rest] = items;
  return foldLeft(folder, folder(item, accumulation), rest);
}

    So I am using notFound() in app/(website)/category/[category]/page.tsx. And even with a not-found.tsx at app/not-found.tsx it is not rendering the not-found.tsx. In that page.tsx I use notFound() in two places, once in the generateMetaData()

    export async function generateMetadata({
  params,
}: {
  params: { category: string }
}): Promise<Metadata> {
  const category = await getCategoryBySlug(params.category)

  if (!category) {
    notFound()
  }

  return {
    title: `${category.title}`,
    description: `${category.excerpt}`,
    openGraph: {
      title: `${category.title}`,
      description: `${category.excerpt}`,
    },
  }
}

    and once in the actual Page()

      const category = await getCategoryBySlug(params.category)

  if (!category) {
    notFound()
  }

    What I found interesting is that the notFound() in generateMetadata() gives me the error (as seen in the screenshot)

    However, if I comment that out and allow it to get to the notFound() in Page() I get a white screen with <html id="__next_error__"> and I am on "next": "^13.3.2", but it also happens on "next": "^13.3.3-canary.1",.

    bpossolo May 1, 2023

    -

    it doesn’t make sense to call notFound() in generateMetadata().

    notFound() is meant to be used in a page handler to return 404 status code and not found content.

    Just return an empty object for the metadata so that none of the meta elements get set.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    All reactions

    Comment options

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

    borispoehland

    -

    With the App directory, how do you make the following combination from the pages directory work?

    • use getStaticProps with ISR. When someone hard routes to this URL, the revalidation will happen, if stale. BUT, via soft navigation, the data served can be stale. That's why I always used this approach in combination with...
    • useSWR, putting the data from getStaticProps as fallbackData, and fetching the freshest data on the client's side

    Now, with the RSC approach, is there an equivalent that does not require useSWR. I assume that the revalidation from the { next: { revalidate }} will also only revalidate on hard mount, not on soft mount. Hence, is there a way to tell "revalidate also on soft routing"?

    Overall I like the new structure. One thing I'd like to see is the ability to customize headers in SSR routes, not just API routes. For example, if there's a page that represents a sporting match and the scores are server-side rendered, I'd like to set the max-age to something like 1 minute when the match is in progress, and 30 minutes post-match. Something like this would be ideal:

    export async function getResponseHeaders(response) {
    const matchStatus = await getMatchStatus();

    response.set('cache-control', `public, s-maxage=${isInProgress ? 60 : 1800}`);
}

export default function MatchPage(props) {
    // render the page here
}

    interface PageResponse {

  status: number;
  headers: HttpHeaders;

}

export default MyPage(props: Props): Promise<PageResponse> { … }

    that way it would allow for the handler to generate the component response + seo metadata within the same function (they often need the same data) along with http response headers and status code

    corneldm
    May 2, 2023

    -

    I have some feedback on the File-Based Metadata API.

    I'm loving the conventions, but I ran into an issue with /favicon.ico resulting in a 500 on production. My favicon is in /app and gets the metadata tag as designed. The problem I encountered, however, happens when building for production remotely. The production server expects to find the favicon.ico file in a path that was present on the build server, resulting in "Error: EACCES: permission denied, open '/build/server/path/to/app/favicon.ico'". I'm temporarily working around this by mirroring the production directory structure on the build server.

    resthedev
    May 4, 2023

    -

    Feedback on parallel + intercept routes

    I was able to get a basic version of a "either open in modal or separate page based on current route" interaction. Overall I'm glad that is now possible natively without super hacky code.

    I had some suggestions for improvements:

    • Preserve scroll of the page while the intercept slot is shown (modal in my case); I saw on Twitter that this was actively being worked on, so I'm looking forward to it in a future update
    • Currently nested intercepts doesn't seem to work for me. For example I cannot intercept using (...)posts/[collectionId]/[postId]. I get an error saying that I cannot use the same slug name. For now, I'm getting around this by avoiding nesting and combining the ID's into one like this: (...)posts/[fullId]
    • When using the useParams hook within my intercept slot, I'm seeing the params of the previous route rather than the current route (the intercepted one). Is this intended behavior?
    • Would be good to get more documentation and examples of use cases for parallel routes

    Thank you team!

    Feedback + Question

    I'm currently using appDir with Mui and its <ThemeConfig /> component which uses Emotion.
    Unfortunately I have to put "use client"; in all my files because of an Emotion bug

    So I can't use Metadata or generateMetadata because of the "use client";
    Next's <Head> component also doesn't work in the appDir version.

    How can I change metadata in a component using "use client"; ?

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    You must be logged in to vote

      • Uh oh!

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

      • Uh oh!

      There was an error while loading. Please reload this page.

    bpossolo May 4, 2023

    -

    you don't generate metadata in client components.
    you do it in the same file as your page handler.
    it might help if you think of things from a "multi-page, server-rendered" perspective...

    • http request arrives, your page handler generates the head metadata and html response.
    • user clicks a link or routes to a new location via some other mechanism like a button click...
    • new http request to page handler which then generates new head metadata & html

    to use mui components in your server rendered pages, you need a wrapper which is marked as 'use client'

    example

    // /about/page.tsx

export metadata: Metadata = {
  canonical: 'https://whatever.com/about'
};

export default function AboutPage(): JSX.Element {
  return (
    <SomeContent />
  );
}
    // some-content.tsx
'use client'
import { Typography } from '@mui/material';

export default function SomeContent(): JSX.Element {
  return (
    <Typography>stuff</Typography>
  );
}

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    leerob
    May 4, 2023
    Author

    -

    App Router Stability

    Hey folks, exciting news. The Next.js App Router is now stable!

    We couldn't have gotten without the literally thousands of replies in this discussion helping us uncover bugs, patch issues, and iterate on our design based on your feedback. We greatly appreciate it. Our focus for the coming weeks will be on fixing any additional bugs that come up during this new wave of feedback.

    Now that the router is stable, there are more additions we want to make, many based on the feedback shared here. To help make it easier to track feedback and upvote or discuss specific feature requests moving forward, we're going to close out this discussion.

    We've made a new section in Discussions specifically for the App Router where you can open new discussions and continue the conversation.

    Frequently Asked Questions

    These are from the 13.4 blog post, but reposting here for clarity.

    What does App Router stability mean?

    Marking the App Router as stable today does not mean our work is done. Stability means that the core of the App Router is ready for production and has been validated by both our own internal testing, as well as many Next.js early adopters.

    There are still additional optimizations we'd like to make in the future, including Server Actions reaching full stability. It was important for us to push towards core stability to help provide clarity for the community on where they should begin learning and building applications today.

    The App Router is built on top of the React canary channel, which is now ready for framework adoption of features like Server Components. Learn more.

    What does this mean for the Next.js beta docs?

    Starting today, we recommend building new applications with the App Router. The Next.js beta documentation, which has been used to explain the App Router and re-written from the ground up, is now merged back into the stable Next.js documentation. You can now easily toggle between the App or Pages Router.

    We recommend reading the App Router Incremental Adoption Guide to learn how to adopt the App Router.

    Is the pages directory going away?

    No, the pages directory is not going away. We are committed to supporting pages/ development, including bug fixes, improvements, and security patches, for multiple major versions moving forward. We want to ensure developers have enough time to incrementally adopt the App Router as they're ready.

    Using both pages/ and app/ in production together is supported and encouraged. The App Router can be adopted on a per-route basis.

    Does this mean Server Components are "complete"?

    Next.js is one framework that is choosing the build on the React architecture, which includes Server Components. We hope that the experience provided with the App Router will encourage other frameworks (or new frameworks) to consider using this architecture, as well.

    There are still patterns yet to be defined in this ecosystem, like handling infinite scroll. For now, we recommend using client solutions for these patterns while the ecosystem grows and libraries are created or updated.

    Beta Was this translation helpful? Give feedback.

    Server actions/mutations looks really great! I have two questions.

    1. Can I use glob pattern in revalidatePath, revalidateTag? e.g, revalidatePath('https://3rdparty.api/pathname/**/*'), revalidateTag('some-category-*'). or do I have to list all things? if possible, regexp could be another option.
